Stop Using Count() > 0 to Check if a Collection Has Items
As developers, we often write code that works without stopping to ask whether it’s the clearest or most efficient way to solve the problem. Over time, these small decisions shape the quality of our codebase.
One pattern I still see in many C# projects is checking whether a collection contains elements using Count() > 0.
if (users.Count() > 0)
{
// Process users
}There’s nothing technically wrong with this code. It produces the expected result and has been used in countless applications. However, if your goal is simply to determine whether at least one item exists, there is a better option.
if (users.Any())
{
// Process users
}At first glance, the difference seems insignificant. Both expressions return a boolean value, and both allow the program to continue only when the collection isn’t empty. But the choice between them is about more than syntax. It reflects intent, readability, and, in many cases, performance.
Writing Code That Expresses Intent
Good code should communicate its purpose as clearly as possible.
When another developer reads Any(), the intention is immediately obvious. You're asking a simple question: Does this collection contain at least one element?
On the other hand, Count() is designed to answer a different question: How many elements are in this collection?
Even though comparing that count to zero eventually gives the same answer, you’re using a method intended for counting when you’re really performing an existence check. Choosing the API that matches your intention makes the code easier to understand and easier to maintain.
What Happens Behind the Scenes?
The real difference becomes apparent when you’re working with sequences that are evaluated lazily, such as LINQ queries over IEnumerable<T>.
Imagine you have a sequence containing one million records.
var users = GetUsers();If you write:
users.Any();LINQ begins reading the sequence and stops as soon as it encounters the first element. Once it knows the answer is true, there's no reason to continue.
This behavior is known as short-circuiting, and it’s one of the reasons Any() is so efficient for existence checks.
Now consider this:
users.Count() > 0;If the sequence doesn’t already know how many elements it contains, LINQ must enumerate every item to calculate the total count before it can compare that number with zero.
For a small collection, the difference may be negligible. For large datasets or expensive queries, that unnecessary work can become significant.
LINQ Queries Make the Difference More Noticeable
The distinction becomes even clearer when you’re working with filtered sequences.
Suppose you want to know whether there are any active users.
Many developers write something like this:
users.Where(u => u.IsActive).Count() > 0Although it works, the query evaluates every matching element before returning a result.
A better approach is:
users.Any(u => u.IsActive)This version communicates your intent more clearly while allowing LINQ to stop as soon as it finds the first matching record.
The code is shorter, easier to read, and often more efficient.
What About Lists and Arrays?
This raises an interesting question.
If you’re working with a List<T> or an array, does the same advice still apply?
Not exactly.
Collections such as List<T> already store their size internally, so accessing the Count property is an O(1) operation.
users.Count > 0Notice that this is the Count property, not the LINQ extension method Count().
The distinction matters.
users.Count > 0
users.Count() > 0
users.Any()The first line simply reads an existing value. The second invokes LINQ, which may need to enumerate the sequence depending on its type. The third performs an existence check.
Even when the performance difference is minimal, many experienced developers still prefer Any() because it describes the intention more accurately. The code says exactly what it means.
Entity Framework Makes This Even More Important
If you’re using Entity Framework, choosing the right method can also affect the SQL generated by your application.
Consider this query:
context.Users.Any(u => u.IsActive)Entity Framework translates it into a query that checks whether a matching row exists. Once the database finds one matching record, it can stop searching.
Now compare it with:
context.Users.Count(u => u.IsActive) > 0Here, the database counts every matching row before comparing the result with zero.
If all you need is to know whether at least one record exists, asking the database to count every matching row introduces unnecessary work.
Choosing the Right Tool
None of this means Count() is a bad method.
It exists for an important reason.
When your application genuinely needs the total number of elements, Count() is exactly the right choice.
var totalUsers = users.Count();
Console.WriteLine($"Total users: {totalUsers}");In scenarios involving reporting, pagination, analytics, or statistics, counting is the correct operation.
The important lesson is to choose the method that matches the question you’re trying to answer.
If you’re asking, “How many items are there?” use Count().
If you’re asking, “Does at least one item exist?” use Any().
Final Thoughts
One of the habits that separates experienced developers from beginners is paying attention to small details.
Replacing Count() > 0 with Any() won't suddenly transform your application's performance. In many situations, the difference is barely measurable.
What it does improve is clarity.
Your code becomes easier to read because it expresses exactly what you’re trying to accomplish. In situations involving LINQ queries, deferred execution, or database access, it can also avoid unnecessary work.
These small improvements rarely make headlines, but they accumulate over time. A codebase filled with thoughtful decisions is easier to maintain, easier to review, and often performs better without requiring complex optimizations.
Clean code isn’t just about making software work.
It’s about making your intent obvious to the next person who reads it — even if that person is you six months from now.
